title: Pi Day: Math Gets Deliciously Round!
story: Every March 14, math lovers everywhere celebrate Pi Day because the date matches the first three digits of pi: 3.14! This special number helps us measure circles, and people mark the day by eating circular treats like pies, pizzas, and cookies. Schools host pi recitation contests where students try to memorize as many digits as possible after 3.14159... Bakeries sell special pies with math puns, and teachers organize fun geometry activities. Some people even time their celebrations to start at 1:59 PM, matching even more digits of pi. It's the perfect excuse to make math tasty and turn numbers into a party!
activity_question: Why is Pi Day celebrated on March 14th?
activity_choices: Because the date 3/14 matches the first digits of pi
Because circles were invented in March
Because it's when pizza was first made round
activity_answer: Because the date 3/14 matches the first digits of pi
bonus_fact: The record for memorizing pi digits is over 70,000 numbers!

title: White Day: Sweet Revenge Time!
story: White Day flips Valentine's Day upside down! On March 14, exactly one month after Valentine's Day, it's the boys' turn to give gifts. The rule is simple: return the favor with something three times more expensive! Some boys spend weeks planning the perfect white-wrapped present to show their appreciation.

Spend half of what you received
activity_answer: Spend three times more than what you received

story: Vroom! A future racing star zoomed into the world on March 14, 1927. Bill Rexford was born in Conewango Valley, New York. He grew up to become a speed demon on the racetrack. In 1950, at just 23 years old, Rexford vroomed his way to victory. He won the NASCAR Grand National Series championship, becoming the youngest Cup champion ever!
activity_question: What racing series did Bill Rexford win?
follow_up_question: How old was Bill Rexford when he became the youngest Cup champion?

title: Revving Up Music: Dieter Schnebel's Unique Sound
story: Vroom, vroom! Can you imagine motorcycles as musical instruments? German composer Dieter Schnebel did! Born on March 14, 1930, in Lahr, Germany, Schnebel became famous for his experimental music. He even created a piece called 'Harley-Davidson' that used nine motorbikes and a trumpet to make an unusual symphony of sounds.
activity_question: What unusual instruments did Dieter Schnebel use in his piece 'Harley-Davidson'?
follow_up_question: How do you think a concert with motorcycles would sound?

title: A Musical Pioneer's Unique Sound Journey
story: Takehisa Kosugi, born in Tokyo on March 14, 1938, would become a groundbreaking violinist and composer. He explored new frontiers in experimental music, creating sounds that pushed boundaries. Kosugi's innovative work with groups like Group Ongaku and Taj Mahal Travellers inspired musicians worldwide to think differently about what music could be.
activity_question: What type of musician was Takehisa Kosugi?

title: Astronaut's Journey Begins
story: A future space explorer took his first breath in Miami, Florida. William Benjamin Lenoir was born on March 14, 1939. He grew up to become an American astronaut, flying on the space shuttle mission STS-5. Lenoir's journey from Earth to the stars shows how dreams can soar to incredible heights!

title: Jona Lewie: Kitchen Party Maestro
story: Jona Lewie, the musical mastermind behind catchy tunes, entered the world in Southampton, England in 1947. Born on March 14, this British pop singer-songwriter and multi-instrumentalist would go on to create hits like You'll Always Find Me in the Kitchen at Parties and Stop the Cavalry. Lewie's clever lyrics and musical talents have kept fans dancing for decades.

Bohemian Rhapsody and We Will Rock You

title: A Step Towards Freedom in Canada
story: In 1793, a brave act sparked change. A black woman, forced into slavery, was taken to New York to be sold. This event moved Lieutenant Governor Simcoe of Upper Canada to action. On March 14, he passed a law limiting the import of slaves. It was a small but important step towards ending slavery in Canada.
activity_question: What did Lieutenant Governor Simcoe do on March 14, 1793?
activity_choices: He passed a law limiting the import of slaves
He freed all slaves in Upper Canada
He became the governor of New York
He wrote a book about slavery
activity_answer: He passed a law limiting the import of slaves
follow_up_question: Why do you think laws against slavery were important?

title: A Park is Born in San Francisco
story: Imagine a giant sandbox turned into a lush green paradise! That's what happened in San Francisco when the California legislature approved an act in 1870. This decision on March 14 paved the way for creating Golden Gate Park. Today, it's a beautiful oasis where families picnic, kids play, and everyone enjoys nature in the heart of the bustling city.
activity_question: What did the California legislature approve in 1870?
activity_choices: An act making Golden Gate Park possible
activity_answer: An act making Golden Gate Park possible
bonus_fact: Golden Gate Park is even bigger than New York's Central Park!

title: Paws and Applause at Crufts
story: Tails wagged and crowds cheered as the world's biggest dog show, Crufts, moved to a new home in 1996. On March 14, the famous canine competition opened its doors at the National Exhibition Centre in Birmingham. Dogs of all shapes and sizes strutted their stuff, hoping to follow in the pawprints of Joshua, the Irish setter who won top honors the previous year.
activity_question: Where did Crufts move to in 1996?
follow_up_question: What breed of dog was Joshua, the previous year's winner?

title: Rugby Star Breaks Point-Scoring Record
story: Cheers erupted as Hazem El Masri kicked his way into rugby history. In 2009, the Canterbury winger became the highest point scorer ever in Australian Rugby League. During a match on March 14, El Masri's penalty kick in the 33rd minute pushed his total to 2,178 points, surpassing the previous record of 2,176.
activity_question: What position did Hazem El Masri play?
bonus_fact: Rugby league is different from rugby union. Both are popular in Australia.
